episodes.title: Private Markets at an Inflection Point
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Goldman Sachs' capital solutions and external investing heads discuss private markets at an inflection point, with the post-2022 rate shock having gummed up the private equity distribution cycle and raised questions about private credit quality. The macro backdrop — elevated rates, geopolitical uncertainty, and the gap between public and private market performance — is the central driver, with cautious optimism around a normalisation in deal activity and distributions over 2026-2028.

**Key points**
- Private markets grew 6x from post-GFC to 2022; the Fed's ~500bp rate hike stalled activity, with distributions as a share of NAV falling to 8-10% vs. a historical norm of 15-20%.
- Average buyout hold period has stretched to ~7 years (from ~5.5 years); average venture-to-IPO time is now 14 years, keeping liquidity locked.
- Private credit default rates remain around 2% (vs. 10%+ in prior stress cycles); retail participation in private credit grew ~60% p.a. over five years and now represents ~20% of the market, with liquidity-mismatch concerns but no systemic credit problem cited.
- Secondary market volume was ~$250bn in 2025; GS estimates it could reach $500bn over the next 3-5 years.
- Geopolitics (Middle East conflict) and lingering inflation are identified as the key risks keeping rates higher for longer and delaying the IPO/M&A reopening.
- GS sees 2026 deal activity poised for recovery, with potential for distribution levels above the 2021 peak within 2-3 years if macro normalises.

**FX / rates implications**
- The prolonged high-rate environment reinforcing private market indigestion is the same backdrop pressuring EM carry trades — any delay in Fed cuts keeps USD/DXY supported and EM risk appetite in check.
- A recovery in global risk appetite and IPO/M&A activity (the bullish GS scenario) would be unambiguously risk-on, supportive of EM FX carry and compressing EM credit spreads.
- Geopolitical escalation or an inflation resurgence — the key downside risks flagged — would extend USD strength and weigh on high-beta EM currencies (BRL, COP, IDR, PHP).

**Watch list**
- Fed rate path: any re-acceleration of inflation or Middle East escalation delaying cuts is the swing factor cited most explicitly.
- IPO pipeline build and large 2026 listings as a barometer of risk-on confidence returning.
- Private credit retail-vehicle redemption trends as a potential early signal of broader credit stress.

episodes.title: Credit Crunch: AI IPOs, Tech Supply and Credit Without Peace
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Bloomberg Intelligence credit strategists discuss the structural shift in global fixed income markets — high yields after a decade of ZIRP, tight spreads, AI/hyperscaler-driven supply, and the growing divergence between inflation-driven rates volatility and resilient corporate credit fundamentals. The key risk is an asymmetric flip from an inflation narrative to a growth/recession narrative, with high yield spreads sub-250bp pricing in no downturn. For EM traders, the read-through is primarily via the USD/DXY channel, US Treasury volatility, and global risk sentiment.

**Key points**
- US IG yields briefly hit 5%, US HY crossed 7.5%; both levels historically attract strong credit buying. European IG still lags US yields materially, reducing the foreign bid into EUR credit.
- USD appreciated during the current war/geopolitical episode — a reversal of the 2025 dollar depreciation that had supported EUR credit inflows.
- Hyperscalers (Google, Amazon) actively diversifying debt issuance away from USD into EUR, GBP, JPY, CHF, CAD; Euro IG tech exposure grown from ~€30bn to ~€112bn in 5 years, with room to double to ~€250bn.
- Bloomberg indices adding B-class 4 tech sub-sectors effective 1 August; pharma/healthcare becoming standalone B-class 3 — passive rebalancing flows to watch.
- EU recession probability at ~35% per Bloomberg terminal model; markets not pricing a growth downturn. Stagflation risk flagged specifically for Europe/UK (growth problem + fiscal problem simultaneously).
- High yield spreads ~245-250bp OAS in Europe — richest vs. IG; a growth shock could deliver 50-60bp+ of correction. Security selection alpha preferred over beta at current spread levels.
- Structural higher-yield regime expected to persist: demographics, de-globalization, fiscal deficits crowding out corporates (sterling IG net supply ~£10bn vs. Gilts ~£135-140bn in 2025).

**FX / rates implications**
- USD strength during geopolitical stress is reducing the EUR credit foreign bid — a headwind for EUR asset outperformance and a reminder that the 2025 dollar weakness that supported EM carry may not repeat.
- Higher-for-longer US Treasury yields (rates volatility as a structural feature) sustain upward pressure on EM funding costs and sovereign spreads; the positive rates-credit correlation during rapid sell-offs is an EM risk-off amplifier.
- If global growth concerns intensify and HY spreads re-price 50-60bp wider, expect broader EM credit spread widening, risk-off flows into USD, and pressure on high-beta EM FX (BRL, COP, IDR).
- European fiscal deterioration and potential UK stagflation keep GBP under structural pressure; EUR/GBP dynamics worth monitoring as a risk barometer for European growth sentiment.

**Watch list**
- Bloomberg BI Q2 investor survey results (end-June) — watch for recession vs. inflation as driver of credit returns.
- Bloomberg index rebalancing effective 1 August (tech sub-sectors, pharma reclassification) — potential passive flow impact on spreads.
- US/European recession probability trackers; any acceleration above 35% would be the trigger for the spread correction scenario.
- Further hyperscaler EUR issuance — supply pipeline a technical headwind for EUR IG excess returns.

episodes.title: Rupture and Resilience
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
PIMCO's 2026 secular outlook — "Rupture and Resilience" — frames the next five years as a wide-distribution macro environment driven by geopolitical fragmentation, an AI capex boom (~$15 trillion globally), and fiscal strain in advanced economies. Fat tails in both directions: disinflationary if AI productivity delivers; inflationary if geopolitical shocks and supply-chain rewiring dominate. Critically for EM traders, PIMCO makes an explicit secular USD weakness call and flags EM local-currency yields as "among the most compelling in over a decade."

**Key points**
- Middle East conflict = "one of the biggest oil supply shocks in history"; short-term inflationary, longer-term demand-destructive as growth slows
- USD to remain dominant reserve currency but "valuation may gradually adjust as global portfolios rebalance"; dollar weakness is historically the strongest tailwind for EM local FX returns
- China exerts persistent disinflationary pressure on global goods prices; limited fiscal space constrains demand-side stimulus
- Bloomberg US Agg yield 4.71%, Global Agg (hedged USD) 4.75% as of June 4, 2026; PIMCO targets 5–7% in diversified global portfolios without compromising quality
- Credit cycle turning: defaults rising in leveraged/private credit; asset-based finance and EM sovereign/quasi-sovereign hard currency preferred
- EM countries with credible central banks, commodity exports, and supply-chain positioning seeing fundamentals "converge toward, and in some cases surpass" lower-rated DM peers

**FX / rates implications**
- Secular USD softness is a direct positive for EM local FX; commodity exporters (MXN, BRL, COP, CLP, PEN, MYR, IDR) doubly supported by energy and terms-of-trade tailwinds
- Oil supply shock is stagflationary for importers (INR, PHP, IDR, KRW, THB) — watch central bank tolerance vs. growth-protection tradeoffs
- Central banks retain "much more conventional policy space" to cut in a downturn; EM duration attractive where CB credibility is high (RBI, BI, Banxico, BCB, BCCh)
- US fiscal unsustainability pushes higher real rates and term premium into UST long end — periodic bouts of DM risk-off could disrupt EM carry trades

**Watch list**
- DXY trend: secular USD weakness is the key unlock for broad EM local outperformance
- Oil/Middle East: monitor escalation vs. demand-destruction crossover for the inflationary impulse fade
- US fiscal/debt sustainability: PIMCO flags "episodic volatility" as markets periodically refocus on deficits — UST term premium spikes as EM risk-off trigger
- EM sovereign spread convergence vs. DM peers: PIMCO explicitly frames this as a secular opportunity, especially commodity-exporting frontier and IG issuers
